columns.extraction_plan: This column asks for an overall risk judgment of individual criteria of evidence (COE) in randomized controlled trials (RCT). This type of assessment is typically performed by external reviewers or in a meta-analysis of trials, rather than being reported within the primary publication of the trials themselves. The document describes the design, conduct, and results of the STAMPEDE trials but does not include an overall risk judgment or quality assessment of its own evidence criteria.

plans.group_name: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1

columns.column_name: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1
columns.extraction_plan: The "Discussion" section on page 11 states: "combining abiraterone with an effective androgen receptor antagonist, enzalutamide, has no discernible long-term benefit." This sentence distinguishes abiraterone from androgen receptor antagonists. Abiraterone acetate is known as an androgen biosynthesis inhibitor. Considering the first experimental arm (abiraterone trial), the class of agent is "Androgen biosynthesis inhibitor".
columns.column_name_raw: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1

columns.extraction_plan: Extract from text on page 4, 5, 6. The value is mentioned in the 'Results' section on page 6, Table 1 on page 5, and Figure 1 on page 4.

The name of the control arm is "Standard of care". This is explicitly stated in the "Methods" section on page 3: "Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to either standard of care (control group) or standard of care plus abiraterone (in the abiraterone trial) or plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial)." The components of the standard of care are described in the "Methods" section on page 3: "The standard of care was androgen deprivation therapy with luteinising hormone-releasing hormone agonists or antagonists or bilateral orchiectomy; after implementation of a protocol amendment (version 13; Dec 17, 2015), docetaxel (75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone (10 mg) orally once per day were permitted after starting androgen deprivation therapy but before starting study treatment."

columns.extraction_plan: In Figure 1: Trial profile on page 4, locate the section for the "abiraterone trial". Under "501 assigned to standard of care plus abiraterone", the number of deaths is "293 died". For the "abiraterone and enzalutamide trial", under "462 assigned to standard of care plus abiraterone plus enzalutamide", the number of deaths is "228 died".

In Figure 1: Trial profile on page 4, locate the section for the "abiraterone trial". Under "502 assigned to standard of care", the number of deaths is "371 died". For the "abiraterone and enzalutamide trial", under "454 assigned to standard of care", the number of deaths is "292 died".

The "Methods" section on page 3 states: "We analysed two open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol... Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) ... to either standard of care (control group) or standard of care plus abiraterone (in the abiraterone trial) or plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial). Each trial randomly assigned patients to its own control group; there were no overlapping controls." This indicates that there were two trials, and each trial had two distinct arms (one standard of care/control arm and one experimental arm). Since the control groups were non-overlapping, the total number of distinct arms across both trials is 2 + 2 = 4. This is further confirmed by Figure 1 (Trial profile) on page 4, which shows two arms for the abiraterone trial and two arms for the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.

The hazard ratio (HR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for progression-free survival (defined as failure-free survival) for the treatment arms compared to their respective control arms is reported. - For the abiraterone trial (Standard of care plus abiraterone vs Standard of care): HR 0.34 (95% CI 0.29–0.39); p<0.0001. - For the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial (Standard of care plus abiraterone plus enzalutamide vs Standard of care): HR 0.36 (95% CI 0.31–0.43); p<0.0001.

A hazard ratio is a comparative measure of the treatment effect (treatment vs control). There is no separate hazard ratio reported *for* the control arm itself in isolation. The HRs provided describe the effect of the treatment arms relative to their respective control arms. Therefore, this specific metric (a hazard ratio *of* the control arm) is not reported. While survival curves for the control arm are shown in Figure 4C and 4D, specific PFS rates (percentages at time points) or median PFS times for the control arm are not explicitly stated in the text.

It is reiterated on page 4 in the "Outcomes" section: "The primary outcome was overall survival for both trials (defined as the time from randomisation to death from any cause)."

The "Outcomes" section on page 4 explicitly lists the primary and secondary outcomes measured in the study, which include overall survival, prostate cancer-specific survival, failure-free survival, progression-free survival, metastatic progression-free survival, symptomatic skeletal-related events, toxic effects and adverse events, and cost-effectiveness. A Quality of Life Scale is not mentioned as one of the assessed outcomes.

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Synchronous
columns.extraction_plan: The study population is defined as patients with metastatic prostate cancer starting androgen deprivation therapy. This implies synchronous metastases, as patients were recruited based on the presence or absence of distant metastases at the start of treatment. This is stated in the "Methods" section on page 3, column 1: "The protocol recruited patients with advanced prostate cancer starting androgen deprivation therapy stratified by the presence or absence of distant metastases on conventional imaging... within 8 weeks of random assignment." The entire reporting of outcomes is for this synchronously metastatic population.
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Synchronous

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Metachronous
columns.extraction_plan: The PDF does not report results based on prognostic groups with metachronous metastases. The study focuses on patients with metastatic disease at the start of androgen deprivation therapy (synchronous).
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Metachronous

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | High volume
columns.extraction_plan: Results are reported based on prognostic groups with high volume of metastases. This is explicitly stated in the "Statistical analysis" section on page 5, column 1: "Given that metastatic volume is prognostic, outcomes split by low-volume and high-volume disease are of interest and were included as an additional prespecified subgroup analysis, in line with other trials of combination therapies in this setting." Figure 3E on page 8 presents the overall survival subgroup analysis for high-volume disease.
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | High volume

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Low volume
columns.extraction_plan: Results are reported based on prognostic groups with low volume of metastases. This is explicitly stated in the "Statistical analysis" section on page 5, column 1: "Given that metastatic volume is prognostic, outcomes split by low-volume and high-volume disease are of interest and were included as an additional prespecified subgroup analysis, in line with other trials of combination therapies in this setting." Figure 3D on page 8 presents the overall survival subgroup analysis for low-volume disease.
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Low volume

columns.extraction_plan: The secondary endpoints are reported in the "Outcomes" section on page 4, within Chunk 7. Extract the text that lists the secondary outcomes: "Secondary outcomes were prostate cancer-specific survival (defined as the time from randomisation to death from prostate cancer); failure-free survival (defined as biochemical failure, local or lymph node progression, distant metastases, or death from prostate cancer); progression-free survival (defined as failure-free survival but excluding biochemical failure); metastatic progression-free survival (defined as progression of or new distant metastases or death from prostate cancer); symptomatic skeletal-related events (defined as pathological fracture or spinal cord compression or bone pain requiring radiotherapy or surgery); toxic effects and adverse events; and cost-effectiveness of research treatment."

The document defines "biochemical failure" as "PSA progression" (page 3, Procedures section). However, it does not report the median time to PSA progression (TTPSA) as a standalone outcome. The paper reports hazard ratios for "failure-free survival," which is a composite endpoint that includes biochemical failure, but it does not provide median times specifically for biochemical failure or PSA progression.

The document defines "biochemical failure" as "PSA progression" (page 3, Procedures section). However, it does not report the median time to PSA progression (TTPSA) as a standalone outcome. The paper reports hazard ratios for "failure-free survival," which is a composite endpoint that includes biochemical failure, but it does not provide median times specifically for biochemical failure or PSA progression.

The total number of participants is reported in the "Results" section on page 6, and visually represented in Figure 1 on page 4. The paper focuses on two trials for metastatic prostate cancer. The abiraterone trial included 1003 patients, and the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial included 916 patients. Summing these two groups gives the total participants for the two trials reported: 1003 + 916 = 1919.

The names of the experimental treatment arms are reported in the "Background" and "Methods" sections of the text, and also as column headers in Table 1. Page 1: "standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally" and "abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial)". Page 5-6: "Standard of care plus abiraterone" and "Standard of care plus enzalutamide and abiraterone".

For the "Abiraterone trial", extract the number of patients in the "Standard of care plus abiraterone" arm from Figure 1: Trial profile on page 4. The value is 501. For the "Abiraterone and enzalutamide trial", extract the number of patients in the "Standard of care plus abiraterone plus enzalutamide" arm from Figure 1: Trial profile on page 4. The value is 462.

Extract the description of the first experimental treatment arm from the "Background" section of the abstract on page 1, which details the regimen for the "abiraterone trial". This is further elaborated in the "Procedures" section on page 3.
The treatment classes are reported in the "Methods" section under "Randomisation and masking" on page 3, and also summarized in the "Background" section on page 1.

The document reports on two distinct randomized phase 3 trials conducted under the STAMPEDE platform protocol. These trials are consistently named throughout the paper. 'Abiraterone trial' is found in the title on page 1 (text), in the 'Background' section on page 1 (text), in the 'Methods' section on page 3 (text), and as a column header in Table 1 on pages 5 and 6, and as a figure title in Figure 2 (A, C, E) on page 7 and Figure 4 (A, C, E) on page 9. 'Abiraterone and enzalutamide trial' is found in the title on page 1 (text), in the 'Background' section on page 1 (text), in the 'Methods' section on page 3 (text), and as a column header in Table 1 on pages 5 and 6, and as a figure title in Figure 2 (B, D, F) on page 7 and Figure 4 (B, D, F) on page 9.

The general description of the therapy is found in the title of the article on page 1 (Chunk 5): "Abiraterone acetate plus prednisolone with or without enzalutamide for patients with metastatic prostate cancer starting androgen deprivation therapy". More detailed descriptions of the specific treatment arms are provided in the "Methods" section on page 1 (Chunk 5): "standard of care (androgen deprivation therapy; docetaxel 75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone 10 mg orally once per day allowed from Dec 17, 2015) or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial)."
